auditVerdicts.assessment: Audited against chunk_00 (30 posts). Caveat: this chunk is ~1/20 of the corpus the top-25 was built from, so a single absent diction word is weak evidence; I weight repeated within-chunk patterns more than single absences. Within this chunk, only ~6 posts actually CITE specific tells (7872, 2267, 2205, 1667, 1663, 1673); the rest are generic "AI slop" laments, slop-image jokes, weekly AI-newsletter posts, or teacher/job anecdotes naming no tell. Among the genuine citations the ranking is broadly faithful at the very top: em dash is cited in 5 of 6 tell-bearing posts (clearly the #1 tell) and the it's-not-just-X-it's-Y construction in 4 of 6 (clearly #2-tier). Below that the list has problems. The biggest structural flaw is dedup failure: it's-not-just-X-it's-Y is split across at least three rows (#3 "it's not just X, it's Y" 17, #20 quoted-variant 2, #23 "not only X but also Y / not X but Y" 2) and no-contractions is duplicated (#17 "no contractions" 4, #21 "no contractions / overly formal" 2); merging these would materially change the ranking. The single-word diction cluster (delve, unleash, game-changer, tapestry, unlock, elevate, comprehensive) occupies 5+ of the top-15 slots yet draws ZERO citations in this 30-post sample — plausibly real corpus-wide but the chunk suggests these meme-words are over-represented relative to construction/structure tells that authors actually call out in prose. Several tells that authors here explicitly name are missing from the top-25 entirely (see missing list). Net: the top ~3 are trustworthy; the middle is distorted by duplicate rows, by diction words that may be inflated by meme-repetition rather than genuine citation, and by the omission of frequently-cited structural/voice tells.
overcounted: Single-word diction tells as a group (delve #8, unleash #9, game-changer #10, tapestry #15, unlock #19, elevate #22, comprehensive #25): zero of these are cited anywhere in this 30-post chunk, despite occupying 7 top-25 slots; they read like a shared meme-vocabulary that gets repeated/listed rather than independently observed, which inflates post-count vs. tells authors actually point to in real writing

it's-not-just-X-it's-Y is inflated/distorted by being counted as THREE rows (#3 at 17, #20 at 2, #21-adjacent #23 'not only X but also Y / not X but Y' at 2) instead of one merged tell — splitting deflates its true single-tell rank while the duplication clutters the list
no-contractions is double-counted (#17 'no contractions' 4 + #21 'no contractions / overly formal' 2) — same tell, two rows

uniform/robotic sentence rhythm (#2, 24 posts) is likely padded by counting generic 'robotic / bland / soulless / reads like a robot' laments (e.g. posts 134, 140, 158, 186, 188) as rhythm citations; in this chunk only post 2205's 'recognizable cadence' is a clean rhythm citation, so #2 at 24 looks high relative to the concrete evidence
unleash (#9, tied 8 posts): not cited once here; a classic listicle meme-word whose count may come from being copied around 'AI words' lists rather than genuine independent observation
undercounted: empty summary sentence that restates the prompt / 'in conclusion...' / 'to summarize the above points' (#16, only 4 posts): cited in TWO of the six tell-bearing posts in this single chunk (1673 lists two variants, 1663 'a summarizing conclusion'), suggesting it is far more frequently cited corpus-wide than its rank-16 / 4-post tally implies

em dash (#1, 43): if anything UNDER-counted — appears as a cited tell in 5 of 6 tell-bearing posts here (7872, 2267, 2205, 1667, 1663); its dominance over everything else may be even larger than 7.1% suggests

as-an-AI-language-model artifact (#11, 7): the broader 'leftover/giveaway boilerplate' category (here 'As an AI developed by OpenAI', plus the demonstrated leftover assistant offer 'Want me to tweak the tone...') is a recurring, high-salience giveaway that authors treat as the ultimate proof; may be under-weighted as a category
missing_important: generic / soulless / 'says nothing' / no real opinion — a content-level tell cited in posts 2205 ('a Lot of Words that said absolutely nothing', 'bland and insincere') and 2267 ('everything feels so right and soulless but without an opinion'); frequently named yet absent from top-25

'straight up', 'era', 'honestly?', 'that's growth' — explicitly enumerated as tells in post 2267 and demonstrated in its own closing line ('honestly? that's not an issue that's growth'); none appear in the top-25

hypophora / rhetorical-question-then-immediate-answer ('And the worst part?', 'the difference is stupid:') — named outright as a top tell in post 1663 and demonstrated in post 5 ('Wait. What if it could?'); a distinct construction missing from the list
purple prose / overwrought adjectives ('stunning, robust, purplish prose') — named in post 1663; distinct from diction-word tells

overuse of transition phrases / 'consistent cohesive sequencing' and 'good variety of transition phrases' — named in 1663 and implied by 1671 (students 'removing transitions' to evade detectors); a structural tell absent from top-25

leftover assistant meta-offer ('Want me to tweak the tone to be more serious, humorous, or confrontational?') — demonstrated verbatim at the end of post 1673; a high-confidence giveaway artifact not represented

scare-quotes around examples / 'lots of quotes for examples' and the both-sides 'on the one hand... but other people...' hedging structure — both named in post 1663; minor but genuinely cited and absent

auditVerdicts.assessment: Audit of the TOP-25 against chunk_04.txt (37 posts). The ranking is directionally right at the very top (em dash is genuinely the most-cited tell, confirmed in 4 distinct posts: lines 30, 104, 188, 228) but is distorted by two systemic problems visible in this chunk. (1) DEDUP FAILURE: several tells are split across multiple rows, inflating apparent breadth while diluting rank. "it's not just X, it's Y" appears twice (#3 and #20), "no contractions" twice (#17 and #21), and "formulaic essay shape" overlaps #5 plus the "sounds formulaic" complaints; these should be merged. (2) USAGE-vs-CITATION CONFLATION: the diction tells (game-changer, deep dive, delve, comprehensive, unleash) are inflated because a string-match counts every appearance, but in chunk_04 most appearances are NOT citations. "game changer" (lines 36, 98) is used sincerely by human authors; "deep dive" (38) and "comprehensive" (40) appear only inside quoted AI output the author mocks, not as named tells; delve/unleash/tapestry/in-today's-fast-paced-world cluster in just two sources — the duplicated "write naturally" prompt list (169-184, 207-222, the SAME post twice) and one satirical demonstration post (56-74) that performs a dozen tells without naming any. Counting demonstration/parody and duplicated prompt-lists as independent citations overstates the whole diction tier. By contrast, sycophancy/positivity is the most under-served relative to how often authors actually flag it here: it is explicitly cited in at least 4 posts (193-196 'great insight!'/congratulate/agree politely; 238-239 'agrees with everything'/'yes-man'; 279 'you're right - that's on me!'; 336 'weirdly optimistic'), rivaling em dash for citation density in this chunk yet sitting at #4. Two genuinely-cited tells are missing from the top list entirely (gender-neutral 'his/her' paired-pronoun artifact, and the 'Here's...' opener that a poster self-flags), and the sycophantic-apology / hallucination tells are under-represented.
overcounted: game-changer (#10) — in chunk_04 it is used sincerely by human authors (lines 36, 98), not cited as a tell; only appears as a named giveaway inside one duplicated prompt list
deep dive / dive in (#6) — appears mainly inside quoted AI output being mocked (line 38) and the duplicated prompt list, not independently flagged
comprehensive (#25) — only appearance (line 40) is inside a quoted AI sample, never named as a tell by an author

delve (#8), unleash (#9), tapestry (#15), in today's fast-paced world (#18) — concentrated in just two sources (a duplicated prompt-list post and one satirical demonstration), so independent-citation count is inflated
it's not just X, it's Y — split across #3 and #20 (same tell counted twice); genuinely cited only once verbatim (line 277)
no contractions — split across #17 and #21 (duplicate of same tell)
unlock (#19) / unlock the power-potential (#14) / elevate (#22) — no genuine author citation in this chunk; rank likely propped up by template/parody text elsewhere
undercounted: positivity/sycophancy (#4) — explicitly cited in ~4 posts here (193-196, 238-239, 279, 336); rivals em dash for citation density in this chunk yet ranked 4th

perfectly-structured / formulaic essay shape (#5) — reinforced by multiple independent complaints (107 'sounds formulaic', 188 'formulaic to the bone', 193 'same polished structure', 342 'overly-polished corporate voice') that are scattered rather than consolidated
empty/soulless polished tone — recurring author complaint (188, 193, 342) not well captured by any single existing row
missing_important: gender-neutral 'his/her' paired-pronoun artifact — explicitly cited as the giveaway in the r/Teachers note post (lines 228, 233); a concrete, high-signal AI artifact absent from the top 25
'Here's...' / 'Here's the thing' opener — self-flagged as an AI tell by a poster (line 301: 'gosh I sound like AI ... I get triggered when reading Here's'); also opens the parody (62)
sycophantic apology / 'you're right - that's on me!' walk-back — cited at line 279 as characteristic AI behavior; distinct from generic positivity and currently uncaptured
hallucination / 'makes stuff up' — named as ChatGPT's core tell (line 238); a content-level tell missing from a list that is almost entirely surface-form
corporate buzzwords / marketing-hype / fluff (unnecessary adjectives) — repeatedly cited (176, 183, 245, 342-344) as a category, broader than the individual diction words listed
'and honestly?' as an injected AI tic — explicitly named alongside 'it's not X, it's Y' at line 277

auditVerdicts.assessment: Audited chunk_08.txt (30 posts, scores ~196-220) against the TOP-25. Caveat: this chunk skews heavily toward META-discussion of AI ("AI slop," detection arms race, teacher/professor frustration, dead-internet) rather than itemized lexical tells, so it's a weak corroborator for diction items and a strong surface for behavioral/structural tells. Only a handful of posts cite concrete writing tells. The clearest corroborations in-chunk: em dash (2 posts: r/ChatGPTPro "Noticing GPT prose style," r/ChatGPT manager/writer), "it's not just X, it's Y" (1: "Noticing GPT prose style," verbatim), rule of three / three-part structures (1: manager/writer, "uses three-part structures constantly"), and uniform/robotic rhythm (1-2: "Noticing..." cites "a specific rhythm," and the bypass-detection post frames burstiness/perplexity as the thing AI lacks). These four are genuine and well-placed. Beyond those, almost none of the top-25 DICTION items (delve, unleash, game-changer, tapestry, unlock, comprehensive, elevate, "in today's fast-paced world," "as an AI language model," emoji bullets) appear as cited tells anywhere in this chunk. Meanwhile the chunk surfaces several tells the top-25 omits or fragments: an entire post dedicated to the opener "Honestly...", a post dedicated to the word "resilience"/"quiet"/"quiet resilience," deliberately-inserted fake typos/spelling mistakes to beat detectors, and a recurring verbosity/repetition complaint (marketing briefs "repeated in 5 different sections," 5-page reviews, 6-paragraph emails). Also note two ranking-integrity defects visible in the list itself: #3 and #20 are the SAME tell ("it's not just X, it's Y") counted twice, and #17 ("no contractions") and #21 ("no contractions / overly formal") are near-duplicates; both splits artificially deflate those tells' true totals and waste two top-25 slots.
overcounted: delve (diction) — zero appearances as a cited tell in this chunk despite the chunk being dense with AI-writing complaints; consistent with delve being a meme people repeat rather than actually cite
tapestry (diction) — absent here
unleash / game-changer / unlock / unlock-the-power (diction) — none cited in this chunk
dive in / deep dive (diction) — not cited here
in today's fast-paced world (phrasing) — not cited here
as an AI language model (artifact) — not cited here; this chunk is 2023-2026 and the explicit-disclaimer artifact has aged out of how people describe tells
emoji as bullets/headers (format) — not cited as a tell in-chunk (emoji appear in titles ironically, e.g. wolf/raven, but not flagged as a tell)
'it's not just X, it's Y' appears TWICE in the list (#3 and #20) — the #20 row is double-counting the #3 tell
no contractions appears twice (#17, #21) — likely inflating slot count via a near-duplicate
undercounted: rule of three / triads (#12, only 7 posts) — explicitly and emphatically cited here ('It uses three-part structures constantly'); for a tell named spontaneously without prompting it is likely under-counted relative to diction memes

uniform/robotic sentence rhythm (#2) — corroborated here via the burstiness/perplexity bypass prompt and 'a specific rhythm', suggesting it is real and possibly even more central than diction items below it

no contractions / overly formal (#17/#21) — the formal/hedging-register tell is real here (verbatim 'I understand that the syllabus carefully outlines...', 'I acknowledge the difficulty...') but its true weight is hidden by being split across two rows

empty/profound-but-hollow phrasing (#16 empty summary) — close kin cited twice here ('phrases meant to sound profound but aren't', restaurant 'word salad' flattery); the family is bigger than its rank suggests
missing_important: Opener 'Honestly, ...' — an ENTIRE top-scoring post (r/SideProject, score 220) is devoted to it ('The word is dead. It's been zombified by AI'); a sentence/paragraph-opener tell absent from the top-25

Specific overused diction 'resilience' and 'quiet' / 'quiet resilience' — a whole r/Professors post built around it; these are concrete lexical tells the list misses while listing rarer ones like tapestry

Verbosity / overlong / repetitive output — recurring across multiple posts (marketing briefs 'same instructions repeated in 5 different sections,' 5-page review of an 8-page article, 6-paragraph student emails); a major behavioral tell with no top-25 slot
Deliberately-inserted fake typos/spelling mistakes to evade AI detectors (r/Professors 'excyted', 'annownce') — a distinct anti-detection artifact not represented
Forced/awkward transitions (cited in r/ChatGPT manager post: 'The transitions are forced') — a structural tell missing from the list
Em-dash-swapped-for-colon evasion variant (manager post: 'switch out their em-dashes for colons') — a notable wrinkle on the #1 tell worth tracking as people adapt

Generic flattery / 'word salad' replies (restaurant-review post: 'adds to the charm', 'evoke memories of childhood and travel souvenirs') — sycophancy-adjacent but distinctly the hollow-compliment pattern, only loosely covered by #4

auditVerdicts.assessment: Audit basis: chunk_12.txt holds 35 posts. The vast majority are ABOUT AI generally (online ed, code automation, "SEO is dead," AI-slop complaints, detectors, model comparisons) and cite NO specific writing tell. Only ~5-6 posts actually name a concrete textual marker, so this chunk is a thin but revealing sample for judging the global TOP-25. The single most important finding concerns counting method: em-dash. This chunk contains 14 literal em-dash characters, but ~13 of them are em-dashes USED as punctuation inside posts (5 as bullet lead-ins in the PromptEngineering tier-list, 3 in the Fable refactor post, 2 in the ChatGPT-sentient rant, etc.). Exactly ONE post cites the em dash AS a tell — "The Scarlet Em Dash" satire — and that post ironically contains zero actual em-dash characters (its body uses trailing "..." ellipses). If the tally's counting conflated "em dash present in post" with "post cites em dash as a tell," then #1 (43 posts, 7.1%) is structurally and badly inflated; in this chunk the genuine-citation-to-incidental-use ratio is ~1:13. By contrast, several real, repeatedly-described tells in this chunk are absent from the top list entirely: fake-relatability/conversational-filler openers ("Look, I get it...", "Imagine this...", "Is that familiar, I have been there too"), trailing-ellipsis "..." style, fabricated/hallucinated citations ("totally made up references"), rigid repeated template across sections, and generic repetitive jargon. The duplicate split entries (#3 vs #20, #17 vs #21) also show the taxonomy is fragmenting counts rather than reflecting distinct citation behavior. Overall the ranking captures the right vocabulary but likely overweights surface-detectable formatting (em dash, bullets) over the relational/rhythm tells authors describe in prose, and is contaminated by counting incidental usage as citation. The chunk is too AI-topic-heavy (vs tell-focused) to validate the lower diction ranks, but the head of the list is the clear problem.
overcounted: em dash overuse (#1, 43 posts) — in this chunk ~13 of 14 em-dash occurrences are incidental punctuation inside posts, not citations; only 1 post ('The Scarlet Em Dash') actually flags it as a tell, and that post has no literal em dash. Strong sign the count conflates presence with citation and is heavily inflated.

everything turned into bullet lists (#7) — the PromptEngineering and Nano Banana posts USE bullets/markdown but do not cite bullets as a tell; risk of presence-counted-as-citation, same failure mode as em dash.

bolded lead-in bullet points (#24) — appears as formatting (bold ** lead-ins in the tier-list and microsaas posts) but is never named as a tell by any author here; likely counts formatting usage as a citation.

no contractions / overly formal split (#17 and #21 are near-duplicates) — formality/robotic tone IS cited (r/ClaudeAI 'formal, robotic and dumb'), but splitting it across two ranks inflates apparent breadth while the contraction-specific claim isn't actually made in this chunk.

'it's not just X, it's Y' (#3 and #20 are duplicates of the same tell) — the double-listing double-counts; no post in this chunk uses or cites this construction, so its #3 rank is unsupported by this sample.
undercounted: uniform/robotic sentence rhythm (#2) — supported here ('formal, robotic and dumb'; 'inhuman and repetitive'), but the description undersells the closely-related repetitiveness/sameness complaints authors actually voice; likely under-credited because it's described in varied prose rather than a fixed phrase.

positivity/sycophancy (#4) — the Fiverr beta-read post describes a rigid 'praise, criticism, suggestions' formula with generic praise; sycophancy-adjacent signal exists but is being captured under structure instead, so the true sycophancy signal is probably under-counted.

perfectly-structured/formulaic essay shape (#5) — clearly under-counted: the Fiverr post ('exact same rigid three-paragraph formula') is one of the strongest, most vivid tell-citations in the whole chunk, yet formulaic structure sits below em dash; rhythm/structure complaints in prose are systematically losing to keyword-matchable formatting tells.
missing_important: Fake-relatability / conversational-filler openers — explicitly enumerated in the 'ChatGPT speech everywhere' post: 'Look, I get it...', 'Imagine this...', 'Is that familiar, I have been there too.' A distinct, concretely-cited tell with no home in the top 25.
Trailing-ellipsis '...' style — central to both 'The Scarlet Em Dash' (whole body is '......' ellipses) and the ChatGPT-speech post; recurs as a marker yet is entirely absent from the list.

Fabricated / hallucinated references and citations — cited in r/Professors ('totally made up references') and echoed in the framework posts ('hallucinated'); a high-signal tell of AI text missing from the ranking.
Generic repeated jargon / repetitiveness — 'same generic writerly jargon over and over again... repetitive' (Fiverr post); distinct from sentence rhythm and not represented.

Rigid identical template repeated across sections — 'every single chapter's feedback followed the exact same rigid three-paragraph formula'; a structural sameness tell separate from 'formulaic essay shape' that the list doesn't capture.
'syntactic mad-lib style' — a named pattern in the r/Professors colleagues post describing AI's fill-in-the-blank sentence construction; no top-25 entry covers it.

best_quotes: Em dashes have become the single most reliable tell of AI-generated text
I can hear the em dashes through your microphone as you talk
the “it wasn’t x, it was y” shit, the excessive rule of three, overuse of meaningless similes and paradiastoles, the weird overexplaining
You're absolutely right! This observation isn't just smart — it shows you're operating on a higher level
It uses three-part structures constantly. The transitions are forced, and the inspiration is fake. Even when people get clever and switch out their em-dashes for colons... sorry, it's still obvious
Paragraphs that start with "Honestly, ..." The word is dead. It's been zombified by AI.
AI writes word salad - beautiful looking but has no nutritional value.
In a world where the tapestry of human emotions unfolds like a delicate symphony, I'm sorry, but as an AI language model, I cannot delve into unlocking your full potential
If it's a “5 ways to…” post, “7 signs you might be…” or a bullet-pointed life guide, there's a solid chance it came from us. We love structure.
no grammar mistakes on essays that use elevated language but ultimately say very little
Same kind of phrases, same sentence patterns, same boring transitions, same flat tone.
a series of bullet point **bolded** Every Word Capitalized colon 2-3 sentences
4.6 now constantly throws in those horizontal line separators (---) throughout the text. It's another obvious AI writing marker
Default Claude hedges everything. "It depends on your needs." "There are several approaches."
synthesis.category_summary: Format and phrasing tells dominate genuine citations; diction (single meme-words) is over-represented relative to how often authors actually point to it. The clearest, most-cited tells are a punctuation tell (em dash) and a construction tell (it's not X, it's Y) — both surface-detectable AND repeatedly named in prose, which is why they top both passes. Below the head, the most TRUSTWORTHY signal is phrasing/structure that authors describe in their own words (robotic rhythm, sycophancy, formulaic shape, forced transitions, hallucinated citations), while the diction tier (delve, unleash, tapestry, game-changer, unlock, elevate, comprehensive) is inflated by listicle/prompt-list copying and parody demonstrations rather than independent observation — all five auditors flagged this. Artifacts (as an AI language model) are high-salience but aging out. Format tells (bullets, bolded lead-ins, emoji, horizontal rules) are real but at risk of presence-counted-as-citation inflation.

final_ranking.note: Both passes' #1 and confirmed cited in 4-5 posts per audited chunk; reads as AI because the punctuation is 'too polished' for casual typing — use commas, periods, or parentheses, and don't just swap in colons (people now flag that too).

final_ranking.tell: "It's not just X, it's Y" / "not X, but Y" construction (merged with #20, #23 variants)
final_ranking.note: True total is higher than 2.8% once the three split rows are merged; the antithesis cadence is THE 'AI accent' — just state the thing plainly instead of negating-then-asserting.
Cited via 'specific rhythm'/'syntactic mad-lib' complaints but partly padded by generic 'sounds robotic' laments; vary sentence length and let some sentences run long or stop short.
Likely UNDER-ranked — Auditor 2 found it rivals em dash for citation density; drop the flattery and reflexive agreement, disagree or stay neutral when warranted.
Under-counted per Auditors 2 and 4 ('formulaic to the bone', 'exact same rigid three-paragraph formula'); break the intro-body-body-body-conclusion mold and let structure follow the idea.

final_ranking.tell: Everything turned into bullet lists / listicles
final_ranking.note: '5 ways to…' / '7 signs…' scaffolding screams AI; use prose paragraphs and reserve bullets for genuinely list-like content.
Confirmed in cliche-avoidance quotes but some appearances are mocked AI output, not citations; cut the metaphor and just start the topic.
Likely under-ranked — named spontaneously across several chunks ('uses three-part structures constantly'); avoid reflexive three-part lists and parallel triplets.

final_ranking.tell: Empty summary / polished-but-hollow phrasing ('says nothing', word salad)
final_ranking.note: Under-counted per multiple auditors (folds in 'soulless', 'no opinion', 'word salad'); reads as AI because it's fluent but contentless — make a real claim and cut the restated-prompt filler.
SOURCE 2 keyword pass puts this higher (2.8%, n=220); the **Bold:** then sentence pattern is a giveaway — write normal sentences without the boldface label.

final_ranking.tell: "As an AI language model" / leftover assistant boilerplate (e.g. 'Want me to tweak the tone?')
final_ranking.note: The ultimate proof when present, but aging out as people stop pasting raw output; delete any meta-offer or disclaimer before publishing.

final_ranking.tell: Single-word diction memes: delve, unleash, tapestry, game-changer, comprehensive, elevate (cluster)
final_ranking.note: Inflated — all five auditors found near-zero genuine citations; counts come from listicle/prompt-list copying, but still real corpus-wide; swap for plain words you'd actually say.
Novel tell surfaced by 4 auditors ('totally made up references'), no SOURCE 1 percentage; a content-level giveaway — verify every source, never invent author names or case law.

final_ranking.tell: "Honestly, ..." / fake-relatability openers ('Look, I get it', 'Imagine this')
final_ranking.note: Novel (no SOURCE 1 percentage), but a whole top-scoring post is devoted to 'Honestly' being 'zombified by AI' (3 auditors); cut the throat-clearing opener and start with the actual point.
Marketing-flavored and split across two rows; reads as hype — say what the thing actually does.
Repeatedly cited as a category broader than single words ('This revolutionary product will transform your life'); strip promotional adjectives and state plain facts.

final_ranking.tell: Verbosity / repetition / restating the same point across sections
final_ranking.note: Novel tell (no SOURCE 1 percentage) raised across 3 chunks ('repeated in 5 different sections', 5-page review of an 8-page article); cut length and say each thing once.
Novel structural tell (no SOURCE 1 percentage; 'The transitions are forced'); the connective tissue is too even — let ideas jump and trim 'moreover/furthermore' scaffolding.
True total higher once merged; stiff register reads as AI — use contractions and write the way you talk.

final_ranking.tell: Hedging / both-sides balance ('it depends', 'on one hand... on the other')
final_ranking.note: Cited as Claude 'hedges everything' giving a menu instead of committing; take a position rather than listing every option.

final_ranking.tell: "In today's fast-paced world" / dated scene-setting opener
final_ranking.note: Iconic but low citation count; a hollow opener — delete it and start with something specific.

final_ranking.tell: Emoji as bullets or section headers
final_ranking.note: Real but not cited as a tell in several chunks; decorative emoji-bullets look templated — use plain text headers.
Novel (no SOURCE 1 percentage), from a single vivid quote ('another obvious AI writing marker'); the markdown line breaks read as generated — use paragraph breaks instead.

final_ranking.tell: Deliberately-inserted fake typos to beat detectors
final_ranking.note: Novel anti-detection artifact (no SOURCE 1 percentage; 'excyted', 'annownce'); ironically itself a tell — don't fake errors, just write naturally.
SOURCE 2 keyword pass puts it higher (~1.0%); the signposted wrap-up is formulaic — end on a real last point, not a recap.

synthesis.reconciliation_notes: The two passes AGREE strongly on the head: em dash is #1 in both (SOURCE 1 7.1%, SOURCE 2 4.5%), and 'it's not just X, it's Y', bullets, game-changer, dive in, and delve appear high in both. They DISAGREE on diction weight and on what counts as a citation: SOURCE 2's deterministic regex inflates words that double as ordinary human prose (it explicitly buckets however/thus 6.3%, nuanced 2.3%, leverage 2.1%, comprehensive 1.6% as LOW-CONFIDENCE because the regex matched the poster's OWN writing, not a cited tell) — so I did NOT promote those into the ranking despite high raw rates. The biggest cross-source disagreement is em dash counting: Auditor 4 argues #1 is badly inflated because ~13 of 14 em-dash occurrences in his chunk were incidental punctuation, not citations, whereas Auditors 1, 2, 3 independently confirm em dash is genuinely the most-cited tell in their chunks; I kept it #1 but noted the presence-vs-citation tension. All five auditors independently flagged the same dedup failures (it's-not-just-X-it's-Y split across #3/#20/#23, no-contractions across #17/#21, unlock across #14/#19), which I merged — this is the single biggest correction. All auditors also agreed the single-word diction cluster is over-ranked (zero genuine citations in four separate chunks, attributed to listicle/prompt-list copying and parody), so I collapsed it to one entry at #12 and demoted tapestry/elevate/comprehensive out of standalone slots. Where SOURCE 1 has no percentage (novel tells folded in: hallucinated citations, 'Honestly' opener, verbosity, forced transitions, fake typos, horizontal rules), share_pct is set to 0 as a 'no SOURCE 1 data' sentinel (noted in each row) rather than an invented number, and confidence reflects how many auditors raised each. Items resting only on SOURCE 2 sub-rates (bolded lead-ins, 'in conclusion') are labeled as such in their notes.
